text:
## What it means if you're unrepresented

Understanding proportionality helps when deciding what to raise at each hearing and how to frame it. Courts expect submissions to be focused and relevant. Raising too many issues at once, or issues that are peripheral to the core dispute, can reduce the time available for the matters that actually need to be decided — and may create an adverse impression.

The practical question at each stage is not "what is everything I want the court to know about?" but "what does this hearing need to decide, and what evidence or argument is necessary for that?" Knowing the purpose of each hearing — what a FHDRA is for, what an FDA is for — is part of being able to use court time effectively.

If the other party is represented and appears to be running a disproportionate or oppressive litigation strategy — generating unnecessary applications, raising issues that serve no purpose in the proceedings, or driving up costs — the proportionality principle is one basis on which the court can be asked to intervene. A direct request to the judge to manage the case more actively, with reference to the overriding objective, is a legitimate step.

<!-- discovery gap: to be completed after research conversations. Expected input: how unrepresented people experience the proportionality principle in practice — for example, whether they feel shut down when they try to raise issues, or whether they are aware the court has discretion to limit what is heard. -->
